next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

Marcus was sitting in the vinyl chair closest to the door when Carmen came down the hall at quarter past midnight. That was wrong. Marcus didn't sit in meetings — Marcus paced, shifted his weight, leaned against walls and pushed off again like a man who couldn't afford to stop moving. But tonight he was sitting with his hands on his knees, still, contained, and that was how Carmen knew he'd come to ask for something.

The TV room was cold. October had arrived while facilities management debated switching the heating system from its summer settings, and the result was a building that held the day's chill instead of releasing it. The overhead fluorescents were off. They used the television's standby light and a brass lamp Agnes had brought from her room months ago, its shade throwing amber across the card table and leaving the rest of the room in blue-gray shadow. The single-pane windows were black, and when Carmen passed close to one she could feel the cold radiating off it like the opposite of warmth, a presence rather than an absence.

Harry was at the table in his wheelchair, though he'd pushed back from it by a few inches — not close enough to work, not far enough to be out of the circle. A calculation in furniture. Agnes sat beside him with only a coffee cup, no notebooks, no pill organizer. Her hands wrapped around the cup with a grip that suggested she already knew what this meeting was about. Earl was against the far wall, arms crossed, legs extended in his institutional slippers. Next to him, Burt's empty wheelchair was folded and leaned against the ba...
open_hooks_to_carry: Lola losing track of where the act ends — genuinely forgetting words, unsure if it's performance or Parkinson's
Tomás's death from pancreatic cancer — the real version, not the euphemized one

payoffs_to_land.description: The warm daily relationship between Lola and Tammy, established in Ch 1, is now the source of Lola's deepest guilt — she's deceiving the person closest to her

prev_tail_excerpt:
...thing hurt — she reached for an action, for a kindness she could perform with her body instead of sitting with a feeling she couldn't fix.

"You don't have to," Carmen said.

"I know, mija."

Lola left. Her footsteps were soft in the hallway, the shuffle that was part performance and part the Parkinson's and part seventy-nine years of weight on the same pair of feet. Carmen listened until the sound blended into the building's nighttime hum and disappeared.

Earl stood. He didn't say goodnight or good morning or anything at all. He walked out of the TV room and back toward 116A, toward his bed three feet from Burt's bed, toward the roommate whose breathing he'd learned well enough that he'd woken up when it changed, when the body hit the floor, when the sound went wrong. Earl had gotten out of bed and crossed the room and grabbed a towel and pressed it to the wound and pressed the call bell with his other hand, and that wasn't the ring's logistics man protecting an asset. That was a man taking care of the person next to him because there was no one else awake to do it.

Carmen and Harry and Agnes sat in the TV room. The lamp buzzed. The wind had died and the parking lot was still and the leaves had settled wherever the last gust had pushed them.

"You said 'us,'" Harry said. Quietly. Not as leverage, not as a weapon he'd hold and use later. Just the observation of a man who counted everything — pills, dollars, margins, risks — and who had counted, too, the moment the last holdout stopped holding out.

Carmen looked at him. "Yeah," she said. "I did."

She didn't take it back.
state_deltas_required: Lola's fear that her performance of impairment is merging with actual decline; guilt about deceiving Tammy
the human cost of sustained deception becomes visible from inside; Lola's confusion about where performance ends and self begins introduces doubt about the ring's psychological sustainability
